q J-\q\
COLLEEN CONWAY MCANDREWS
JERRY MARGARET 
SIMMONS
SIMMOIVS & MCANDREWS
ATTORNEYS AND COUNSELORS AT LAW
I44I FOURTH STREET
SAIYTA MONICA, CALIFORNIA 9O4OT

F,
September 27, L992
Ms. Lynda Cassady
PoIitical Reform Consultant
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 8OO
Sacramento, CA 958L4
Dear Ms. Cassady:
This Ietter is to confirm the recent verbal- advice I
received from you pertaining to the lobbying provisions of the
Political Reform Act.
The facts we discussed are surnmarized as foI1ows. A
California nonprofit mutual benefit corporation is in the process
of requesting tax exempt status pursuant to Internal Revenue Code
Section 50L(c) (6) as a business league, but has not yet received
a determination letter acknowledging exempt status. This
relatively new nonprofit corporation has six members all of which
are business entities. 
Each member pays dues to the nonprofit
corporation. These dues are not uniform but rather are based on
the size of the memberts business. Some of these members employ
their own lobbyist and file guarterly Lobbyist Employer Reports.
The nonprofit corporation was organized to promote the common
bu-siness interests of its members including providing marketing
assistance and product and technology development. The nonprofit
corporation plans to attempt to influence legislation and
administrative action which may impact the business interests of
its members. In addition, it is anticipated that the nonprofit
corporation may from time to time make expenditures which would
benefit legislative or state agency officials 
such as mea1s.
These activity expenses would be paid for from the general-
membership dues received by the nonprofit corporation. No member
would exercise direction or control over these activity expenses
nor is there any agreement or mutual understanding that specific
activity expenses will be made. Moreover, at the time the dues
are paid to the nonprofit corporation no member knows or has
reason to know that any particular portion of their membership
dues will be used to make a gift to a specific official 
or
specific group of officials.
a, 
r


Us. Lynda Cassady
September 2l , 1992
Page 2
I posed the fol,lowing questions:
1. 
Does the nonprofit corporation qual-ify as the employer
of a lobbyist despite the fact that it has only six
members, some of which nay already be the employers of
the lobbyist?
2. 
If the nonprofit corporation makes any activity
expenses, will the nonprofit corporation be deemed to
be the maker of these expenses rather than its members?
You advised me that so ]ong as the organization is a bona
fide association it would be deemed to be the empl-oyer of a
lobbyist rather than its individual members. Neither the
Political Reform Act nor the regulations of the Fair Political-
Practices Commission required that there be a specific number of
members before the organization qualifies as a lobbyist employer
pursuant to Governnent code Section a2o39.5 or 2 california code
of Regulations Section 18239.5. You further advised me that any
activity expenses would be attributable to the lobbyist ernployer
rather than the individual menbers.
Thank you for your advj-ce with respect to these issues. rn
the event that r have rnisunderstood or inadvertently misstated
your advice regarding this matter, please contact ne imnediately.
JMS : kLn
Sincerely yours/,--

sections.question:
1. Does the nonprofit corporation qualify as the employer of a lobbyist despite the fact that it has only six members, some of which may already be the employers of the lobbyist?
2. If the nonprofit corporation makes any activity expenses, will the nonprofit corporation be deemed to be the maker of these expenses rather than its members?
sections.conclusion: You advised me that so long as the organization is a bona fide association it would be deemed to be the employer of a lobbyist rather than its individual members. Neither the Political Reform Act nor the regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission required that there be a specific number of members before the organization qualifies as a lobbyist employer pursuant to Government code Section 42039.5 or 2 california code of Regulations Section 18239.5. You further advised me that any activity expenses would be attributable to the lobbyist employer rather than the individual members.
sections.question_synthetic: The inquirer seeks FPPC confirmation of verbal advice regarding whether a six-member nonprofit business league qualifies as a lobbyist employer under the Political Reform Act and whether activity expenses would be attributed to the organization or its individual members.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request/correspondence letter from an attorney to FPPC seeking written confirmation of previously received verbal advice. It is not an FPPC response letter. The document includes the questions posed and the advice given during the verbal conversation, but the FPPC has not yet formally responded in writing. Document quality is fair with some OCR artifacts and formatting issues.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Does the nonprofit corporation qualify as the employer of a lobbyist despite the fact that it has only six members, some of which may already be the employers of the lobbyist?
2. If the nonprofit corporation makes any activity expenses, will the nonprofit corporation be deemed to be the maker of these expenses rather than its members?

CONCLUSION: You advised me that so long as the organization is a bona fide association it would be deemed to be the employer of a lobbyist rather than its individual members. Neither the Political Reform Act nor the regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission required that there be a specific number of members before the organization qualifies as a lobbyist employer pursuant to Government code Section 42039.5 or 2 california code of Regulations Section 18239.5. You further advised me that any activity expenses would be attributable to the lobbyist employer rather than the individual members.

embedding.summary: Attorney Jerry Margaret Simmons writes to FPPC consultant Lynda Cassady to confirm verbal advice regarding whether a nonprofit business league with six members qualifies as a lobbyist employer and how activity expenses should be attributed under the Political Reform Act.
